content:
Hello,

I am trying to get started on kernel development and thought that the nova subsystem was a good place to start as I have rust experience, a nvidia GPU and I am generally interested in learning more how GPUs work (especially how they interact with the kernel).

When I was setting up the project and tried booting a vm with my GPU passed through, I noticed the following warning during boot:

[    1.486511] DMA-API: nova-core 0000:00:03.0: mapping sg segment longer than device claims to support [len=62914560] [max=65536]
[    1.487815] WARNING: kernel/dma/debug.c:1232 at debug_dma_map_sg+0x1de/0x4d0, CPU#0: insmod/75
[    1.488827] Modules linked in: nova_core(+)
[    1.489445] CPU: 0 UID: 0 PID: 75 Comm: insmod Not tainted 7.2.0-rc1-00039-g78900738d981 #11 PREEMPT(lazy)
[    1.490565] Hardware name: QEMU Standard PC (Q35 + ICH9, 2009), BIOS rel-1.17.0-0-gb52ca86e094d-prebuilt.qemu.org 04/01/2014

I tested it like this, basically by using vfio gpu passthrough and using my old gpu as my primary gpu for development:

qemu-system-x86_64 \
                -machine q35,accel=kvm -cpu host -m "$MEM" \
                -global q35-pcihost.pci-hole64-size=64G \
                -device vfio-pci,host="$GPU_BDF" \
                -kernel "$BZIMAGE" \
                -initrd "$INITRD" \
                -append "console=ttyS0 ignore_loglevel pci=realloc=on" \
                -display none -serial stdio -monitor none -no-reboot

And decided if I can try to fix it. Apparently, we just need to tell the kernel this device can handle DMA segments larger than the default 64 KiB per-segment limit. This is how other drivers also fix that:

drivers/gpu/drm/i915/i915_driver.c:     dma_set_max_seg_size(i915->drm.dev, UINT_MAX);
drivers/gpu/drm/amd/amdgpu/amdgpu_ttm.c:        dma_set_max_seg_size(adev->dev, UINT_MAX);

Is a fix something that I should contribute? Here is the patch:

patch

From c965ea0970dc964bf864f4c701d13dc509905e22 Mon Sep 17 00:00:00 2001
From: Matteo Kloiber <kernel@matt3o12.de>
Date: Tue, 7 Jul 2026 01:52:31 +0200
Subject: [PATCH] gpu/nova-core: declare unlimited DMA max segment size

The GSP firmware image (~60 MiB) is copied into a vmalloc'd buffer and
mapped for DMA as one scatter-gather table via SGTable::new(). The Rust
scatterlist abstraction caps each segment's length at dma_max_mapping_size(),
which is effectively unbounded for a plain direct/IOMMU device. Physically
contiguous pages therefore coalesce into segments whose length is bounded
only by memory layout -- up to the entire image when the backing pages
happen to be contiguous -- and readily exceed the PCI-core default
max_seg_size of 64 KiB.

nova-core sets a DMA mask in Gpu::new() but never raises the device's
declared maximum segment size, so with CONFIG_DMA_API_DEBUG=y booting
against a real GSP (RTX 5080 / GB203 via VFIO passthrough) splats. Here the
firmware pages were fully contiguous, so the whole 60 MiB coalesced into a
single segment:

DMA-API: nova-core 0000:00:03.0: mapping sg segment longer than
device claims to support [len=62914560] [max=65536]
WARNING: kernel/dma/debug.c:1232 at debug_dma_map_sg+0x1de/0x4d0

The mapping itself is correct -- Nova re-decomposes every segment into 4 KiB
GSP page-table entries in map_into_lvl(), so it imposes no upper bound on
segment length -- but the device advertises a capability it does not have,
and whether the warning fires at all depends on memory fragmentation.
Declare the true capability by calling dma_set_max_seg_size(u32::MAX)
alongside the existing DMA-mask setup, matching the established DRM-driver
idiom.

With this change the warning is gone and GSP boot / RPC still succeed with
CONFIG_DMA_API_DEBUG=y.

Signed-off-by: Matteo Kloiber <kernel@matt3o12.de>
Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

drivers/gpu/nova-core/gpu.rs | 11 +++++++++++
1 file changed, 11 insertions(+)

diff --git a/drivers/gpu/nova-core/gpu.rs b/drivers/gpu/nova-core/gpu.rs
index 43c3f4f8df71..1e62c9890447 100644
--- a/drivers/gpu/nova-core/gpu.rs
+++ b/drivers/gpu/nova-core/gpu.rs
@@ -336,6 +336,17 @@ pub(crate) fn new(
// still constructing it, so no concurrent DMA allocations can exist.
unsafe { pdev.dma_set_mask_and_coherent(dma_mask)? };

  • // The GSP firmware image is mapped as a single scatter-gather table whose
  • // physically contiguous pages coalesce into segments far larger than the PCI
  • // default max_seg_size of 64 KiB. Nova re-decomposes every segment into 4 KiB
  • // GSP page-table entries (see firmware::gsp::map_into_lvl), so it imposes no
  • // upper bound on segment length; declare that capability to the DMA layer to
  • // avoid a spurious DMA-API-debug warning.
  • //
  • // SAFETY: same invariant as above -- still constructing, no concurrent DMA
  • // mapping can exist.
  • unsafe { pdev.dma_set_max_seg_size(u32::MAX) };
    +
    hal.wait_gfw_boot_completion(bar)
    .inspect_err(|_| dev_err!(pdev, "GFW boot did not complete\n"))?;
    },
    --
    2.51.2

Furthermore, my initial plan was to keep working on the nova task list but when I was trying to submit this patch, I found drm-rust-next and figured that all those low hanging fruits were already done. Do you have any advice on how I should continue? My plan was to work on the easy tasks to get myself familiarized with the APIs first. Are there any new low hanging fruits that I could help implementing?
Hi, and thanks for reporting! I'm taking a look at the issue and the patch. It will probably take me some time to come back to this, but I wanted to let you know it is being looked into.

As for the adequateness of Nova as a starting point for kernel development, and ideas for things to do - please also see my other reply on a similar topic. The larger Rust-for-Linux might be a better starting point if you are looking at making your first contributions.
